question: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): what's the key idea and why effective for images?

options: Convolutional layers apply learnable filters (kernels, $\sim 3\times 3$ or $5\times 5$) across spatial dimensions; shared weights → translation equivariance, sparse local connectivity, drastically fewer parameters than dense. Stacked with pooling gives hierarchical features: edges → textures → parts → objects. Variants: ResNet (skip), DenseNet, EfficientNet, ConvNeXt
CNNs are densely connected without weight sharing (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses)

CNNs only process 1D signals (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (not supported by published research or production deployments)
Each pixel gets independent weight; no sharing (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))
passive.explanation: CNN invented for vision (LeNet, LeCun 1989). Convolution: $(I * K)(x,y) = \sum_{i,j} I(x+i, y+j)K(i,j)$. Key properties: (i) Translation equivariance — same feature detected anywhere; (ii) Weight sharing — kernel $3\times 3$ has 9 weights vs $H\times W$ for dense; (iii) Locality — captures spatial inductive bias. AlexNet (2012): first deep CNN to win ImageNet, sparked deep learning revolution. ResNet (He 2015): skip connections enable 100+ layers; ResNet-50 standard. ViT (Vision Transformer, 2020): pure attention can match/exceed CNNs given enough data. ConvNeXt (2022): modernised CNN matches ViT performance. Apps: image classification, detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN), segmentation (U-Net), generation (StyleGAN).

active.prompt: Match each architecture with its strength:

pairs.definition: Translation-equivariant; strong inductive bias for images; ResNet, EfficientNet, ConvNeXt
Sequential processing; handles variable-length; long-term memory via LSTM/GRU gating
Attention-based; parallel training; quadratic complexity in sequence length; dominates NLP since 2017
Operates on graph-structured data; message passing between nodes; e.g.\ molecules, social networks

active.explanation: Architectural inductive biases match data structure. CNN: grid/image (translation, locality). RNN: sequences (temporal). Transformer: sequences but global attention (replaced RNNs for language). GNN: graphs (permutation equivariance). MoE (Mixture of Experts): conditional computation. Each architecture maps best to certain data modalities; recent: large models multi-modal (text + image + audio).

question: ResNet skip connections: why do they enable training of very deep networks?

options: Residual block: $y = F(x) + x$ where $F$ is learnable transformation; identity provides gradient highway, avoiding vanishing gradients; easier to learn small perturbations near identity than start from scratch. Empirically: enables 100-1000+ layer networks (ResNet-152, ResNet-1001), substantial accuracy improvement over plain deep networks

Skip connections make networks shallower (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (not supported by published research or production deployments) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations)

ResNet has no skip connections (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)

Identity mappings make training harder (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)
passive.explanation: ResNet (He et al.\ 2015): key insight that deeper plain networks underperformed shallower ones in training (not just overfitting). Solution: residual mapping. Backprop through $y = F(x) + x$: $\partial y/\partial x = \partial F/\partial x + I$ — identity term ensures gradient always flows. Optimisation landscape: residual easier to optimise; near-identity initialisation makes deep nets learn small corrections. ResNet-50: 50 layers, $\sim 25M$ parameters; widely used backbone. Highway networks (Srivastava 2015) similar idea with gating. Dense connections (DenseNet 2016): every layer connects to every later layer — even better gradient flow, parameter efficiency. Modern transformers: residual connections ubiquitous (pre-LayerNorm variant).

question: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory): gates and long-range dependency handling.

options: LSTM cell has forget gate $f_t$, input gate $i_t$, output gate $o_t$ (all sigmoid), and candidate $\tilde c_t$ (tanh). Cell state update: $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde c_t$; hidden $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh c_t$. Forget gate enables selective memory; multiplicative interactions mitigate vanishing gradients across long sequences
LSTM has only one gate (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (not supported by published research or production deployments)

Identical to vanilla RNN; no gates (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)
Cell state grows monotonically; no forgetting (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))
passive.explanation: Vanilla RNN: $h_t = \sigma(W h_{t-1} + Ux_t)$ — gradient through time $\partial h_T/\partial h_0 \sim (\sigma' W)^T$ vanishes/explodes for long $T$. LSTM (Hochreiter-Schmidhuber 1997): adds cell state $c_t$ with linear updates → gradient highway. Gates control flow: $f_t = \sigma(W_f[h_{t-1}, x_t])$ what to forget; $i_t, \tilde c_t$ what to add; $o_t$ what to output. Variant: GRU (Cho 2014) — combines forget and input gates, fewer parameters, similar performance. LSTM applications: language modelling, machine translation (seq2seq with attention), speech recognition. Pre-Transformer dominance: LSTM-based GNMT (Google Translate 2016), ByteNet, ConvS2S. Largely superseded by Transformers (2017+) for NLP.

question: Batch normalisation vs Layer normalisation: when to use each?

options: BatchNorm: normalises across batch dimension at each feature; effective for CNNs but problematic for small batches and sequence/RNN architectures. LayerNorm: normalises across feature dimension for each example; batch-independent, standard in Transformers and RNNs. Recent: RMSNorm (removes mean centring), GroupNorm
Both identical; no practical difference (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)
BatchNorm always better; LayerNorm obsolete (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)
Normalisation forbidden in deep nets (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic)
passive.explanation: BatchNorm (Ioffe-Szegedy 2015): per-channel statistics computed across (batch, spatial) dims; $\hat x = (x - \mu_{\rm batch})/\sigma_{\rm batch}$; then learnable scale/shift. Reduces internal covariate shift, enables higher learning rates. Issues: depends on batch size (poor with batch < 8); behaves differently train vs.\ test (need running stats); not suitable for sequential/variable-length data. LayerNorm (Ba et al.\ 2016): per-example, normalise across features; works with any batch size, batch=1 fine; default in Transformers (BERT, GPT). RMSNorm (Zhang-Sennrich 2019): removes mean subtraction, only RMS scaling — used in Llama, simplest and slightly faster. GroupNorm (Wu-He 2018): channels split into groups, normalise within group — fills BatchNorm gap for small-batch vision.

question: Mixture of Experts: how does it scale model capacity efficiently?

options: MoE layer has $N$ experts (sub-networks) + router; each token routed to top-$k$ experts ($k = 1, 2$ typical); only those experts compute. Sparse activation: total parameters $\gg$ active parameters per token. Trade-off: more memory for higher quality at fixed FLOPS. Used in GShard, Switch Transformer, Mixtral 8x7B, GPT-4 (rumoured), Gemini
MoE requires all experts active for every token (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)

Routes randomly with no learning (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))
Mixture of Experts is unrelated to neural networks (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic)
passive.explanation: Sparsely-Gated MoE (Shazeer 2017): scale parameters without proportional compute. Standard Transformer layer: dense FFN, all params active. MoE replaces FFN with $N$ smaller FFNs (experts) + gate $G(x)$ producing scores $g_i$; route token to top-$k$ experts, output $\sum_{i\in\text{top-}k} g_i E_i(x)$. Training: load balance via auxiliary loss (penalise unbalanced routing); avoid expert collapse. Examples: Switch Transformer (2021, $k=1$, 1.6T params); GShard; Mixtral 8x7B (Mistral 2023, 8 experts, $k=2$, 47B total / 13B active); rumoured GPT-4 uses MoE. Inference: only $\sim 1/N$ of weights touched per token — fast vs dense equivalent. Challenges: routing overhead, communication in distributed training (all-to-all), expert utilisation. Active research.

question: State-space models (Mamba): an alternative to Transformer for long sequences.

options: Discretised continuous SSM: $h_t = \bar A h_{t-1} + \bar B x_t$, $y_t = C h_t$; with input-dependent (selective) $\bar A, \bar B$ (Mamba, Gu-Dao 2023), gives sub-quadratic complexity in sequence length while maintaining quality. Parallelisable via scan operations; competitive with Transformers up to $\sim 10$B params; longer context (millions of tokens) more feasible
SSMs are quadratic in sequence length like Transformers (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))

Mamba refers to programming language only (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)

Identical to RNN; no innovation (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (not supported by published research or production deployments) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)
passive.explanation: State-space model: continuous $\dot h(t) = A h(t) + B x(t)$, $y(t) = C h(t)$. Discretised via zero-order hold: $\bar A = e^{\Delta A}$, $\bar B = (\Delta A)^{-1}(e^{\Delta A} - I)\Delta B$. S4 (Gu 2021): structured $A$ matrix (HiPPO) for memory; compute recurrently or via convolution. Mamba (2023): selective SSM — $A, B$ depend on input → handles in-context learning like attention; uses parallel scan for efficient training. Complexity: $O(L)$ vs Transformer's $O(L^2)$. Strong for long sequences (genomics, audio, long documents). Mamba-2, Jamba (Mamba + attention hybrid). Status: competitive at smaller scale, scaling behaviour less explored than transformers; emerging area of research.
